data.title: DSO NextJS Starter: one-command bootstrap for non-engineer prototyping
data.description:
Context: Non-engineering users — designers, PMs, and other non-technical stakeholders — want to use DSO to rapidly prototype client demo ideas. Today, bootstrapping a new DSO-ready project requires multiple manual steps: cloning the plugin, running setup scripts, answering a 7-area onboarding interview, and running additional skills before any feature work begins. This creates a barrier that prevents non-engineers from reaching the 'describe what I want to build' moment without help from an engineer. The DSO NextJS Starter removes that barrier: a single curl command on macOS installs all dependencies, scaffolds a pre-configured Next.js project, and opens Claude Code so the user can immediately type /dso:roadmap. The installer scaffolds the project by cloning a public GitHub template repository (created as part of this epic as a fork of navapbc/template-application-nextjs). Both the template repository and the installer script are deliverables of this epic.

Approach: Option A — Fork navapbc/template-application-nextjs as a public GitHub repo under the DSO org; installer script lives at plugins/dso/scripts/create-dso-app.sh in the DSO plugin repo. Script clones the fork via git clone, performs sed-based project name substitution with shell-metacharacter sanitization, installs npm deps, pre-bakes all DSO infrastructure files, and opens claude. Dependency foundation: Homebrew.

Success Criteria:
1. On a macOS machine with Homebrew already installed but no other project-specific tools (Node, git, pre-commit, Claude Code absent), the installer completes all steps and opens Claude Code within 10 minutes measured from the curl command on a connection capable of >=25 Mbps download; running ls in the opened Claude Code session shows the scaffolded project directory.
2. The installer checks for and installs all required dependencies via Homebrew (bash 4+, git, GNU coreutils, Node 20.x via brew install node@20 with explicit PATH injection, pre-commit, Claude Code via brew install --cask claude-code); for each dependency it cannot install automatically, it prints 'Run: brew install <package>' and exits non-zero with 'Prerequisites not met — re-run after installing the above.'
3. The scaffolded project is a valid Next.js application (contains package.json with next as a dependency, an app/ or pages/ directory, and npm run dev starts without errors) pre-wired with DSO infrastructure files (project-understanding.md, design-notes.md, ARCH_ENFORCEMENT.md, dso-config.conf, CLAUDE.md, .pre-commit-config.yaml, .github/workflows/ci.yml) such that invoking /dso:roadmap in the first Claude Code session produces a roadmap prompt without emitting any gate-blocked error.
4. The installer performs project name substitution throughout all template files; project names containing shell-unsafe characters (spaces, slashes, special regex chars) are sanitized or rejected with an actionable error message.
5. Before the plugin installation step, the installer prints a numbered explanation and pauses for acknowledgment; if the user sends EOF/Ctrl-C, the installer exits with code 1 and prints 'Installation cancelled.'
6. The installer uses a .dso-init-complete sentinel file to distinguish fully initialized from partially initialized projects; running the installer in a fully initialized directory exits informatively; a partially initialized directory prompts the user to resume or start fresh.
7. The installer script lives at plugins/dso/scripts/create-dso-app.sh in the DSO plugin repo and is invocable via curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/<org>/digital-service-orchestra/HEAD/plugins/dso/scripts/create-dso-app.sh | bash.
8. An automated smoke-test script (tests/scripts/test-create-dso-app.sh) runs the installer end-to-end in a sandboxed temporary directory with mocked dependency checks, and asserts the expected project structure, substituted project name, and presence of the .dso-init-complete sentinel; the test asserts exit code 0 on newline acknowledgment and exit code 1 on stdin close.

Priority: Value=4 (Significant — directly enables non-engineer prototyping use case), Effort=3 (Moderate — installer script + template fork + pre-baked artifacts) -> P2
